summary: Jeff Atwood examines why phishing attacks remain devastatingly effective despite years of security awareness efforts. Drawing on a 1994 hacking book and a 2006 phishing study, he demonstrates that social engineering exploits fundamental human tendencies that don't change with technology. The study showed that even when users were warned to expect fake websites, 90% fell for well-crafted phishing pages, and no demographic factors correlated with resistance. Atwood concludes that since users will always be fooled by convincing spoofs, the only viable defense is preventing phishing pages from ever being displayed, through browser-based blacklists that block known malicious sites before users can interact with them.
key_points: Social engineering attacks exploit timeless human vulnerabilities—helpfulness, trust, and preference for ease of use—that persist regardless of technological advancement
A 2006 study found 90% of users fell for good phishing sites, with no correlation between vulnerability and education, age, sex, or computer experience
Users consistently ignore security indicators: 23% never checked address bars, and 15 of 22 dismissed fraudulent certificate warnings without hesitation
Traditional security measures like warnings, SSL indicators, and browser chrome are ineffective because users either ignore them or can't interpret them correctly
Browser-based blacklists that block phishing pages entirely represent the only effective mitigation, since any displayed phishing page will fool a significant percentage of users
money_quotes: Users who are in-the-know will always want to help the less fortunate ones who are not. Those who aren't will seek the advice of the gurus. And so social engineering and reverse social engineering live on.
I just wish (sort of) that they would realize what danger they put themselves in every time they act without security in mind.
Neither education, age, sex, previous experience, nor hours of computer use showed a statistically significant correlation with vulnerability to phishing. Everyone was vulnerable.
When presented with a spoofed web page, a large percentage of users will always fall for it. Forever.
It doesn't matter how many security warnings we present, or how much security browser chrome we wrap websites in.
The only security technique that can protect users from phishing scams, it seems, is the one that prevents them from ever seeing the phishing page in the first place.
key_insight: Security measures that rely on user judgment will always fail against social engineering; the only effective protection is preventing the attack from ever reaching the user.
